standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can figure out "how much per one" even when the amounts are messy fractions.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can look at a table or a graph and tell whether two things are truly growing together in a steady, reliable way.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can find the "magic multiplier" that connects two things — whether it's written as a table, a graph, an equation, a diagram, or just described in words.

lcs.shortTitle: Constant of proportionality in graphs, tables
lcs.subject: Math

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can write a simple equation that captures any proportional relationship, so you can plug in any number and get your answer instantly.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can look at any point on a proportional graph and explain in plain English what it actually means — especially the two most important points.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can handle the percent problems that come up in real life — sales tax, discounts, tips, interest — even when they take more than one step.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You understand that two opposite numbers cancel each other out completely — and can spot that pattern in real life.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can add any two rational numbers by thinking of it as movement on a number line — and explain what the result means in real life.

lcs.shortTitle: Interpret p + q on number line
lcs.subject: Math

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You know that subtracting a number is the same as adding its opposite — and can use that to find the distance between any two numbers.

lcs.shortTitle: Distance as absolute value of difference
lcs.subject: Math

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can rearrange or regroup numbers when adding and subtracting to make the math easier — and still get the right answer.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You understand why multiplying two negatives gives a positive — and can interpret what multiplication of signed numbers means in context.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can divide any two rational numbers (as long as you’re not dividing by zero) and know how the signs work.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can reorder or regroup numbers when multiplying and dividing to make the calculation easier.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can convert any fraction to a decimal using long division, and you know the decimal will either stop or repeat forever.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can add, subtract, multiply, and divide with any rational numbers — including negatives and fractions — to solve real problems.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can simplify and rearrange algebraic expressions — combining like terms, distributing, and factoring — even when the numbers are fractions or decimals.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can rewrite a math expression in a different form to make it easier to understand what it’s actually telling you.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can solve messy, multi-step real-world problems using whatever number form works best — fractions, decimals, or percents — and check whether your answer actually makes sense.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can write and solve an equation to find an unknown number in a real-world situation — and explain each step you took.

lcs.shortTitle: Solve px + q = r and p(x + q) = r
lcs.subject: Math

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can solve problems where the answer isn’t one exact number but a whole range — and show that range on a number line.

lcs.shortTitle: Solve px + q > r or px + q < r
lcs.subject: Math

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can use a scale drawing to figure out real measurements — lengths, areas, and distances — and redraw it at a different scale.

lcs.shortTitle: Reproduce scale drawings at new scale
lcs.subject: Math

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can construct triangles and other shapes from given measurements — and know when the measurements make a unique shape, multiple shapes, or no shape at all.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can picture what shape you’d see if you sliced through a 3D object — like cutting through a box or a pyramid at different angles.

lcs.shortTitle: Cross-sections parallel or perpendicular to base
lcs.subject: Math

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can calculate how far around a circle is and how much flat space it covers — and you understand why the two formulas are related.

lcs.shortTitle: Find the area of a circle
lcs.subject: Math

lcs.shortTitle: Find the circumference of a circle
lcs.subject: Math

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can look at a diagram with intersecting or adjacent angles and write an equation to find any missing angle measure.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can find the area of flat shapes, the surface area of 3D objects, and the volume of boxes and prisms — even when the shape is made of smaller pieces combined.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You understand that you can learn about a whole group by studying a smaller sample — but only if that sample was chosen fairly.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can use data from a random sample to make a reasonable guess about a whole population — and you know that different samples give slightly different answers.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can look at two sets of data side by side and judge whether they’re really different, or just randomly varying — using spread to put the gap in perspective.

lcs.shortTitle: Center difference as multiple of variability
lcs.subject: Math

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can compare two groups using both their averages and how spread out their data is, and draw a reasonable conclusion about which group performs better.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You know that probability is a number between 0 and 1 that tells you how likely something is — and you can match everyday language like “rare” or “guaranteed” to that scale.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can estimate the probability of something by actually doing it many times and tracking results — and you know your estimate gets more reliable with more tries.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can calculate the probability of any outcome when all outcomes are equally likely — by counting favorable outcomes and dividing by total outcomes.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can build a probability model from real data when outcomes aren’t equally likely — and use it to make predictions.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You understand that when two things happen together, you need to think about all possible combinations — not just each thing separately.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can systematically list every possible outcome of a multi-step situation using a list, table, or tree diagram — without missing any or double-counting.

standards.subject: Math
standards.plainLanguage: You can design and run a simulation to estimate the probability of a complicated event that’s too hard to calculate directly.
